# E Corp — Staff SWE | Interview Dossier

**Role:** Staff Software Engineer, Platform Intelligence — build AI-powered incident triage and agentic approval workflows for a 340-service financial platform. Staff IC, remote US.

**Outcome:** Accepted 2026-06-12. Start 2026-07-07. $232K base + $15K sign-on + $90K/yr RSU (~$355K Year-1 TC).

---

## Live cue card

**The role in one sentence:** Lead AI-augmented incident automation and developer tooling at E Corp — the kind of RAG + agentic work I've already shipped in production, at 10x the scale.

**Why I fit (say this early):**
- Meridian RAG: 4M-page corpus, sub-60s triage, 200 daily users, ~60% research-cycle reduction
- Meridian agentic workflow: Claude API + custom tools, human-in-the-loop safety, 87% procurement admin cut
- Pulsar: 3 concurrent enterprise deployments, all on schedule, zero P1s, avg ttv 14→9 weeks

**Comp anchor:** Target $240K base + $15K sign-on. Floor $190K — not a negotiating position.

---

## Key questions + grounded answers

**1. Walk me through a system you built that reduced operational toil.**

Meridian RAG pipeline: replaced a 3-hour manual research triage process with sub-60s cited-excerpt queries over a 4M-page internal corpus. Prototype to production in 8 weeks; 200+ daily internal users; approximately 60% reduction in research-cycle time. Key technical unlock: sliding-window chunking + parent-document retrieval — naive sentence-level chunking lost cross-document context and caused hallucinated citations in the early build. Fixed that, shipped the working version, added a citation-verification suite to catch future regressions.

**2. How would you design an LLM-powered incident triage pipeline for a high-volume financial platform?**

Index runbooks, past postmortems, and service topology docs in a vector store — pgvector on existing Postgres to start, HNSW index for sub-500ms retrieval. On alert: extract structured context (service, error class, affected metrics), run semantic search, retrieve top-k chunks, pass with alert context to an LLM, produce a structured triage card (probable cause, severity reasoning, recommended first action, escalation path). Deliver to on-call in Slack in under 10 seconds. Human reviews and acts; the AI layer is strictly read-only and additive — never in the alert delivery path, never auto-executing on write actions.

**3. Tell me about aligning non-technical stakeholders on a risky AI feature.**

Meridian agentic procurement: finance team's default was "no" — they'd seen automation projects fail silently before. I didn't ask for permission. I ran a live demo on 10 of their own already-resolved procurement requests, showed the agent's draft email and budget-code lookup, then showed the human-in-the-loop confirmation step before any send. I invited them to find errors. They found two: ambiguous budget code mappings. We fixed those live. By the end of the session the finance manager asked to expand the pilot. Observable system plus an explicit human gate turns "do you trust AI?" into "do you trust your own judgment?" They always do.

**4. How do you evaluate retrieval quality without a labeled test set?**

Three layers: offline gold-standard (30-50 known-good scenarios, run on a schedule after every corpus update — catches regressions before they hit production); implicit in-production feedback (did the on-call act on the suggestion within 5 minutes, and did they click through on the cited source?); explicit feedback (thumbs-up/down on the triage card in Slack — even 10% response rate on high-sev alerts catches systematic gaps). At Meridian this combo caught a deprecated procurement policy doc that kept surfacing in retrieval; fixed the corpus hygiene, not the model.

**5. How do you mentor as a staff IC without formal management authority?**

Be the person they show the design to before the PR, not after. Office-hours 1:1s where they drive — they bring the problem, I ask questions. Most of the time the person already has 80% of the right answer and needs a thinking partner, not an authority figure. I externalize my own design reasoning: short design docs, "why" comments in code rather than "what." If I get the same category of question twice, I build the document or pattern that handles it — not another 1:1 answer. The goal is to remove myself as the bottleneck, not become one.

---

## Questions I asked

1. What does success look like at 6 months — what's shipped and what's measurably better?
2. Is this team starting greenfield, or inheriting existing infrastructure I'd own from day one?

---

## Study notes (pre-onsite skim)

- E Corp AI-ops initiative is greenfield within a mature infra org. Public SRE blog mentions "zero-ticket incident response" as the north star — tie answers to that.
- Tech stack pointers: pgvector + HNSW for retrieval; Claude or OpenAI API for completion; Python primary in the team.
- Human-in-the-loop pattern: draft → confirm → execute. Never auto-execute write-side actions (email sends, infra changes, permission grants).
- Alert deduplication: (service_name, error_class) keyed sliding window; count is the severity proxy, not noise volume.
- Eval harness: offline gold-standard regression suite + implicit in-production feedback + periodic explicit thumbs-up/down. Run offline suite on a schedule after every corpus update or prompt change.
- Distributed systems concepts: multi-region failover, HNSW approximate nearest-neighbor, p99 latency at burst load, graceful degradation when AI layer is unavailable (always additive, never in the critical path).

interviewDossier.markdown:
## Hooli — Principal Engineer, AI Platform

**Role:** Own architecture + delivery of Hooli's multi-tenant LLM orchestration layer; lead 4–6 eng, cross-team with ML Research and product orgs.

---

### Live cue card

**Comp anchor:** $270K base / ~$490K TC run-rate offered 2026-06-09. Floor $190K base. Offer declined 2026-06-13 — location constraint.

**5 likely questions + Riley's grounded answers**

**1. "Walk us through a production AI system you've owned end-to-end."**
Meridian RAG: 4M-page corpus, prototype→prod in 8 wks, 200 daily users, ~60% research-cycle-time cut. Owned ingest pipeline, retrieval ranking, citation UX, and prod ops. Go-live used a golden-test-set validation gate in the ingest pipeline — caught two near-incidents before they shipped.

**2. "Design a multi-tenant LLM orchestration layer."**
Three layers: Gateway (auth, tenant ID, quota check), Orchestration (routing, RAG, tool-use), Fleet (model endpoints). Quota = dual token-bucket per tenant (RPM + TPM) in Redis. Routing = logical model names resolved against a fleet registry; model swap is a registry update, not a tenant config change. Canary via weighted routing rule.

**3. "How do you handle multi-tenant isolation in a shared LLM platform?"**
At Pulsar: per-customer config namespacing + credential isolation per enterprise deployment. At Meridian: tenant-scoped vector indices + row-level access gates. Cost attribution: per-request write to timeseries store (not on hot path), feeds dashboard. Interactive calls fast-fail on quota drain (429 + Retry-After); batch calls queue.

**4. "Tell me about a time you led without formal authority."**
At Pulsar ran 3 concurrent enterprise deployments in Q1 — cross-functional w/ CS, Sales, and customer eng teams. No direct reports; aligned through shared runbooks + weekly cadence. All 3 live on schedule, zero P1 incidents. Cut avg time-to-value 14→9 weeks.

**5. "What's your approach to observability for an AI platform?"**
Four signals: request success rate per tenant+model; latency p50/p95/p99 by tier (interactive vs. batch); retrieval quality score trends (if evals exist); rolling cost per tenant vs. quota. On-call triage tree: one tenant or all? one model or all? three root causes I've seen most: quota surprise, model endpoint health, corpus drift without re-index.

**2 questions to ask**
- "What does the AI Platform team own vs. product teams — where's the API boundary today, and how settled is that line?"
- "What's the biggest unsolved scaling problem in the current LLM orchestration stack?"

---

### Study layer

**Hooli context:** Consumer + enterprise products at scale; AI Platform is internal infra, not customer-facing end users. Team owns LLM gateway, retrieval stack, eval/observability layer. Reports to Marcus Webb, VP Engineering AI Platform. Known tension: product teams sometimes route around the platform (build their own retrieval stacks) — be ready to discuss platform governance + adoption.

**Retrieval depth:** Two-stage pipeline: ANN shortlist (Pinecone/Qdrant, top-50), cross-encoder re-rank (top-k, ≤20 candidates to protect latency). HNSW for recall, IVF-PQ for memory. Chunk strategy: 512-token chunks, 64-token overlap. Embedding freshness is the silent killer — the validation harness I built at Meridian is the answer.

**Watch for:** Hybrid SF 2x/week is a real constraint — be direct that it's the deciding factor, not a negotiating chip. Don't say it's fine if it isn't.

**Status as of 2026-06-15:** Offer declined 2026-06-13 in favor of E Corp (full remote + scope). Thread ended amicably; Dana left door open for future remote-eligible roles.

interviewDossier.markdown:
# Final Loop — Weyland-Yutani Staff Engineer, Spaceframe
**Panel:** Jun 14, 10 AM CT | Lena Park (VP Eng), Soren Holt (Principal Eng), Kira Oduya (Staff Eng), Dag Eriksen (Mission Ops)

---

## Role in One Line
Staff Eng owning distributed telemetry + HITL approval stack for mission-critical spaceframe ops — reliability, human-in-the-loop safety, AI-augmented monitoring.

## My Anchors
- **Pulsar:** 3 concurrent enterprise deployments, 0 P1s, time-to-value 14→9 weeks
- **Meridian RAG:** 4M pages, sub-60s queries, 200+ daily users, 8-week prototype→prod
- **Meridian HITL workflow:** Claude API + ERP tools, human-confirm-before-write — that pattern IS what Spaceframe is formalizing

## Comp Anchor
Base ask **$230K** (mid of their $215–255K band). TC ~$340K at mid. Floor $190K absolute. On-call premium fair to request if load exceeds 1 wk/quarter.

---

## 5 Likely Questions + Answers

**Reliability decision under uncertainty (Lena):** Meridian RAG cutover — latency spike on launch day, chose not to roll back. Routed affected query pattern to fallback, communicated proactively to 6 power users, fixed in 4 hours. Lesson: know your fallback before you flip the switch.

**Life-safety system design (Soren):** Fail to safe state, not to action. On-orbit alerting must be independent of the link — local life-safety path has no dependency on ground confirmation. Same principle as Meridian HITL: if confirmation doesn't arrive in 30s, agent does nothing and waits.

**Architecture consensus (Soren/Kira):** Write the RFC. Async debate surfaces better objections than meetings. Reversible decisions: time-box 30 days and measure. Irreversible: explicit decision-maker. I've never had to go above one escalation level.

**High-latency ops (Dag):** Local-first topology. Link is for coordination and post-hoc analysis, not the critical path for immediate safety responses. Question back to Dag: how does the local alerting threshold config get updated and validated?

**Cross-functional reliability (Lena):** Start with 'what does downtime cost you in concrete terms?' Once they quantify it, the redundancy math is obvious. Make residual risk explicit and get sign-off in writing.

---

## Questions for the Panel
- **Lena:** What does success look like at 12 months — what would make you say 'that person changed how we build'?
- **Soren:** On plan-level HITL approval — what's the blocking constraint today: eng capacity, operator UX, or regulatory?
- **Kira:** What's the hardest part of day-to-day engineering that the JD doesn't mention?
- **Dag:** In a real blackout scenario, what does the current system not handle well?

---

## Watch For
- Vague on-call scope (yellow flag)
- Any suggestion the HITL approval layer can be bypassed under operational pressure
- Unclear escalation tree for safety-critical incidents

## Cyberdyne Systems — Staff ML Engineer · Onsite Prep
**The role in one line:** Own model-serving reliability and evaluation pipelines for autonomous systems — production ML conscience of the neural-net division.

---

### Likely questions + grounded answers

**Q: Walk me through a production ML system you owned end-to-end.**
Pulsar scheduling AI — owned the full deployment lifecycle across 3 concurrent enterprise accounts in one quarter. Discovery, integration (Salesforce, Greenhouse, Workday), config, go-live, hypercare. Zero P1 incidents. Cut avg time-to-value 14→9 weeks. The metric was reliability, not model novelty.

**Q: How do you design an eval pipeline for high-stakes model outputs?**
Meridian RAG pipeline over 4M pages: eval was recall + citation precision, human-review gate on any excerpt driving downstream action. Offline eval gives coverage; online sampling tells you if the model is still behaving in prod. For autonomous systems I'd add shadow scoring + confidence gating before any write action.

**Q: Tell me about a time you stopped or delayed a deployment.**
Pulsar, mid-quarter: customer pushed an undocumented schema change. Model eval on new format untested — pulled go-live by one week, ran evals, re-confirmed with customer. Pre-agreed readiness criteria gave me cover to hold without a political fight.

**Q: How do you handle safety vs. product disagreement on a release?**
Set stop criteria before the model is done, not during launch review. At Pulsar I brought readiness criteria into the project kickoff; a hold becomes 'enforcing what we agreed,' not 'engineer slowing things down.' Safety veto should be structural.

**Q: What's your read on agentic systems in production?**
Shipped one at Meridian: Claude API + custom tools parsing vendor quotes, ERP budget lookups, drafting approval emails with human-in-the-loop confirmation before writes. ~87% reduction in procurement admin. Confidence gate on every tool call; full audit log; human step non-negotiable until 3 months of prod data.

---

### Comp anchor
Band $215–255K base / $320–380K TC (posted). Ask $240K+ — hybrid SF is a real concession for an Austin-remote candidate; price it in.

### Questions to ask
1. Who has stop-deploy authority — engineering, safety, or product — and what happens when they disagree?
2. What does the eval pipeline look like today, and what's the biggest coverage gap?

### Study layer
- **Model-serving:** batching (dynamic, request coalescing), canary deploys, P50/P99 latency budgets, rollback automation
- **Eval patterns:** offline evals, shadow scoring, confidence-gate thresholds, outcome feedback loops, human-in-the-loop review

## Live cue card

**Q1 — Walk me through a complex customer integration you owned end-to-end.**
Pulsar + Greenhouse / Workday integrations. I own full lifecycle: discovery, gap analysis, config, integration build, hypercare, sign-off. Three concurrent in one quarter, all live on schedule, zero P1 incidents. Cut avg time-to-value from 14 to 9 weeks.

**Q2 — How did you design the RAG pipeline at Meridian? What was the hardest part?**
4M-page internal corpus. Chunking strategy was the crux — fixed-size first, then shifted to semantic paragraph boundaries because retrievals kept straddling context windows and degrading answer quality. End result: sub-60s cited-excerpt queries vs 3-hr manual triage. 8 weeks prototype to prod, 200+ daily internal users, ~60% research-cycle-time reduction.

**Q3 — Tell me about a time you drove alignment across engineering and non-engineering stakeholders.**
Meridian agentic procurement workflow. Started by mapping the procurement team's existing approval process before writing any code. Built human-in-the-loop confirmation into the tool-call chain — Claude API + custom ERP tools — so finance never lost audit control. Result: ~87% admin time reduction per procurement manager.

**Q4 — System design: design a real-time compression-quality monitoring service for 10K enterprise tenants.**
Event-driven fan-out: compression job emits metric events → Kafka topic → per-tier consumers aggregate P50/P99 latency + quality scores → anomaly detection (Z-score sliding window, 15-min lookback) → alert webhooks or Slack. Tenant isolation via partition key = customer_id. Retain raw events 30 days, hourly rollups 1yr. Multi-tenant dashboard reads from a read replica; writes are append-only.

**Q5 — Why Pied Piper? Why this role now?**
Compression at scale is foundational infrastructure; the AI layer on top — adaptive quality tuning, smart decompression hints, workflow triggers off compression events — is where I'd be building. The FDE and RAG work I've done is exactly the bridge between a technical platform and a customer outcome. I want to keep owning that handoff.

---

## Comp anchor
Target base ≥ $215K / TC ≥ $260K. Posted band $180–215K base. Top of band meets the ask; if the offer comes in mid-band, negotiate base first, citing the posted top. Equity matters but don't let them substitute cash below floor.

## Questions to ask
1. What's the biggest friction point today between PP's compression core and the enterprise customer experience — and where does the AI layer help most?
2. How does the enterprise product team interface with the core-platform team on new API surface? Who drives the contract when they conflict?

---

## Study layer
- PP's compression uses a middle-out algorithm (lossless). Brush up on lossless vs lossy tradeoffs and why enterprise customers care about reproducibility — Bertram Hooper will probe technical vision here.
- Priya Nakamura owns the enterprise API layer and integrations team. Her priority is reliability SLAs and integration velocity.
- Dev Okafor's tech screen focused on retrieval quality and API fan-out; expect the final round to go more abstract: distributed state, multi-tenancy, observability.
- Have Pulsar FDE numbers ready: 3 concurrent deployments, 14 → 9 weeks time-to-value, zero P1 incidents. These are the proof points for 'enterprise-grade AI rollout' at this level.

## Black Mesa — Research Engineer | Loop Stage

**Role in one line:** Build production-grade data pipelines and internal tooling for a research org that runs on Python, Kafka, and ambiguity.

### Likely Questions + Grounded Answers

**1. Walk us through turning a prototype into a production system.**
> Meridian RAG pipeline: 8-week prototype-to-prod on a 4M-page corpus. Started as manual analyst search, became sub-60s cited-excerpt query tool, 200+ daily users. Key moves: instrument first, set SLOs before hardening, write the runbook before go-live.

**2. How do you handle data sources that are poorly documented or constantly changing?**
> At Pulsar, Salesforce/Greenhouse/Workday each had undocumented field mappings. Built a schema-drift detector that flagged mismatches in CI. Treat external docs as a liability, code as truth.

**3. How do you scope work with researchers who can't spec requirements precisely?**
> Start with the pain metric, not the feature request. At Meridian, ran 2 days of observation sessions to define "sub-60s with cited excerpt" as the real target. Prototype exposed the gaps, researchers validated.

**4. Tell me about an on-call incident on a system you owned.**
> Pulsar enterprise deployment — P1-adjacent caught at 2 AM from latency spike alert. Misconfigured Salesforce batch sync window. Fixed, added validator, updated runbook, post-mortem. Zero customer impact.

**5. What's your experience with LLM-powered internal tooling?**
> Meridian agentic workflow: Claude API + custom tools to parse vendor quotes, look up ERP budget codes, draft approval emails with human-in-the-loop confirmation before any write. Cut procurement admin ~87% per manager.

- Posted band: $195–235K base + 10–15% bonus + equity
- Riley's ask: **$215K base**
- Hard floor: **$190K base**

interviewDossier.markdown:
## Massive Dynamic — Staff Engineer
**Panel Interview | 2026-06-20 | Remote (Zoom) | 10 AM–1 PM CT**

---

### Live cue card

**Role in one line:** Staff Engineer on the AI Platform team — own the LLM integration layer; team-lead duties; cross-functional with data science and enterprise customers.

**Q: Walk us through the most complex distributed system you've designed.**
> Meridian RAG pipeline over 4M pages. Key decisions: 512-token chunks + 64-token overlap, hybrid BM25+dense retrieval, cross-encoder reranker top-20→top-5. Built eval harness (LLM-as-judge + human spot-check) before shipping. Prototype→prod in 8 weeks; 200+ daily users; ~60% research-cycle-time reduction.

**Q: How do you handle multi-tenant data isolation in a shared LLM platform?**
> Namespace-level corpus separation per tenant. Metadata filtering applied *before* retrieval (not post-filter — that risks internal leakage). Audit log every retrieval: tenant_id, query hash, retrieved doc_ids. Rate-limit at API gateway, not inside the model call.

**Q: Describe leading a cross-functional initiative without formal authority.**
> Pulsar Q1 2026: 3 concurrent enterprise deployments, 4 customer eng teams + internal infra. Shared status board, weekly 15-min standups, escalation ladder documented upfront. All 3 live on schedule; 0 P1 incidents. Time-to-value 14→9 weeks.

**Q: How do you evaluate retrieval quality continuously?**
> Three layers: (1) offline eval on 500+ labeled query/doc pairs (Recall@5, MRR@5); (2) online A/B on live traffic (click-through, session length); (3) LLM-as-judge sampling 50 queries/day. Regression gate in CI: PR that drops Recall@5 by >2 points blocks deploy.

**Q: Where do you want to be in 3 years?**
> Staff or Principal, still hands-on, owning architecture of a platform multiple product teams build on. Player-coach, not full EM. The best version has me in customer conversations once a quarter and writing production code the rest of the time.

**Comp anchor:** Floor $190K base. Ask $240K. Posted band $215–255K — targeting mid-range; use TC ($320–380K) as the headline number with Marcus.

**2 questions to ask:**
1. "What's the biggest gap between where the LLM integration layer is today and where the roadmap needs it in 12 months?"
2. "How does Staff Eng interface with enterprise customers — do you join discovery or onboarding calls?"

---

### Study layer

**What Riley brings:** Meridian RAG (production, 200+ users, 8-week build) + Pulsar FDE (enterprise deployment lifecycle, 3 concurrent) gives a rare combo of platform depth + customer-facing instinct.

**Gap to address proactively:** ACL change handling was flagged in the system design debrief. If pushed: "I'd tighten the async cache TTL to synchronous invalidation on write for compliance-critical changes — the 60-second window is a tradeoff worth closing given the firm's compliance posture."

**Watch for:** Role is more inward-facing than Riley's FDE targets. Acknowledge honestly — the quarterly enterprise BRs are the feedback loop Riley would lean on.
